findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is an expansile, hypodense mass located within the petrous apex, causing smooth bony expansion and erosion. On magnetic resonance imaging, the lesion characteristically demonstrates bright, hyperintense signal on both T1- and T2-weighted sequences.
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include the absence of central contrast enhancement, although a thin rim of peripheral enhancement may be seen. A dark peripheral hemosiderin ring is also occasionally visible on T2-weighted sequences due to the chronic breakdown of blood products.
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of restricted diffusion to suggest a congenital cholesteatoma. Furthermore, there is no aggressive, permeative bone destruction or adjacent meningeal enhancement that would point toward apical petrositis or a malignant neoplastic process.
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of an expansile petrous apex mass that exhibits intrinsic high T1 and T2 signal due to chronic internal hemorrhage.

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Cholesterol granuloma, given the classic high T1 and high T2 signal without central enhancement. These lesions occur secondary to nonspecific chronic inflammatory changes and are the most common primary lesion involving the petrous apex.
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Mucocele, however mucoceles typically do not exhibit the same degree of bright T1 signal intensity because they lack the chronic macroscopic hemorrhage seen in cholesterol granulomas. Instead, mucoceles generally follow simple fluid signal, appearing T1 hypointense and T2 hyperintense.
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Congenital cholesteatoma, given its propensity to cause progressive bone erosion and potential cranial nerve deficits if left untreated. However, congenital cholesteatomas are classically low signal on T1-weighted images and exhibit striking restricted diffusion, which is absent here.

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to accurately define the relationship of the mass to critical adjacent structures, including the inner ear, carotid canal, and cranial nerves, to guide potential surgical drainage.
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is useful to monitor the growth rate of the expansile lesion and determine if it is starting to impinge on the internal auditory canal or surrounding neural foramina.
management.furtherImaging: I would recommend a dedicated MRI of the internal auditory meatus and skull base without and with intravenous contrast if only a CT has been performed, as this modality is superior here for definitively demonstrating the pathognomonic T1 and T2 hyperintensity of a cholesterol granuloma and assessing for any restricted diffusion.
management.mriSequences: I would specify non-contrast T1-weighted sequences to confirm the intrinsic hyperintense blood products, diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) to confidently exclude a cholesteatoma, and post-contrast T1-weighted images to rule out an enhancing solid neoplasm.
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the Head and Neck / Skull Base MDT. My report would clearly document the size of the lesion, the degree of petrous apex expansion, and the proximity to the internal carotid artery to assist in planning surgical fenestration and drainage if the patient is symptomatic.

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is an expansile, hypodense mass located within the petrous apex, causing smooth bony expansion and erosion. On magnetic resonance imaging, the lesion characteristically demonstrates bright, hyperintense signal on both T1- and T2-weighted sequences. Supporting features include the absence of central contrast enhancement, although a thin rim of peripheral enhancement may be seen. A dark peripheral hemosiderin ring is also occasionally visible on T2-weighted sequences due to the chronic breakdown of blood products. Importantly, there is no evidence of restricted diffusion to suggest a congenital cholesteatoma. Furthermore, there is no aggressive, permeative bone destruction or adjacent meningeal enhancement that would point toward apical petrositis or a malignant neoplastic process. The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of an expansile petrous apex mass that exhibits intrinsic high T1 and T2 signal due to chronic internal hemorrhage.

The appearances are most in keeping with Cholesterol granuloma, given the classic high T1 and high T2 signal without central enhancement. These lesions occur secondary to nonspecific chronic inflammatory changes and are the most common primary lesion involving the petrous apex. The principle differential would be Mucocele, however mucoceles typically do not exhibit the same degree of bright T1 signal intensity because they lack the chronic macroscopic hemorrhage seen in cholesterol granulomas. Instead, mucoceles generally follow simple fluid signal, appearing T1 hypointense and T2 hyperintense. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Congenital cholesteatoma, given its propensity to cause progressive bone erosion and potential cranial nerve deficits if left untreated. However, congenital cholesteatomas are classically low signal on T1-weighted images and exhibit striking restricted diffusion, which is absent here.

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to accurately define the relationship of the mass to critical adjacent structures, including the inner ear, carotid canal, and cranial nerves, to guide potential surgical drainage. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is useful to monitor the growth rate of the expansile lesion and determine if it is starting to impinge on the internal auditory canal or surrounding neural foramina. I would recommend a dedicated MRI of the internal auditory meatus and skull base without and with intravenous contrast if only a CT has been performed, as this modality is superior here for definitively demonstrating the pathognomonic T1 and T2 hyperintensity of a cholesterol granuloma and assessing for any restricted diffusion. I would specify non-contrast T1-weighted sequences to confirm the intrinsic hyperintense blood products, diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) to confidently exclude a cholesteatoma, and post-contrast T1-weighted images to rule out an enhancing solid neoplasm. This should go to the Head and Neck / Skull Base MDT. My report would clearly document the size of the lesion, the degree of petrous apex expansion, and the proximity to the internal carotid artery to assist in planning surgical fenestration and drainage if the patient is symptomatic.
